terms.def: The Sufi invocation recited at sunrise, calling upon the divine qualities of the Creator. One of the foundational daily prayers in the Inayatiyya tradition.
The Sufi prayer of body and heart, performed as a devotional act combining movement, breath, and sacred phrases. Distinct from the Islamic salat.
A closing prayer or seal. In the Inayatiyya tradition, it is recited to close a period of practice or meditation, sealing the spiritual work.
A prayer of gratitude and supplication, offered before meals or at the close of a gathering. Expresses thankfulness and humble petition.
The spiritual journey or path of the wayfarer (salik). In Sufism, it refers to the practical traversal of the spiritual stations and states.
A spiritual guide or teacher. In Sufism, the murshid directs the disciple (murid) on the path, offering guidance through inner realization.
A student or disciple on the Sufi path who has committed to following the guidance of a murshid (spiritual teacher).
An elder or senior spiritual guide. In the Inayatiyya lineage, the Pir is the head of the Order, currently Pir Zia Inayat-Khan.
Remembrance of the Divine through repetition of sacred names or phrases. A central Sufi practice for purifying the heart and awakening divine awareness. Also spelled dhikr.
A specific divine name or sacred phrase given for repetitive practice. Wazifas are prescribed by a teacher for spiritual development of particular qualities.
Contemplation or reflective thought. In Sufi practice, a meditative technique combining breath with a sacred phrase for deep inner absorption.
A breathing practice in Sufism. Refinement of breath used to purify and vitalize the subtle bodies and awaken inner perception.
An occupation or practice of concentration. In Sufism, exercises designed to develop focused attention and inner perception.
Meditation or watchfulness. A Sufi practice of seated contemplation, observing the inner landscape while maintaining attentiveness to the divine presence.
In the Suluk context, the first of four major areas of study: the capacity to gather and direct the mind's attention as a foundation for spiritual development.
The second area of Suluk study: sustained reflective attention that moves beyond concentration into deeper layers of meaning and insight.
The third area of Suluk study: practices of inner stillness, receptivity, and absorption that open the practitioner to direct spiritual experience.
The fourth and culminating area of Suluk study: the direct apprehension of spiritual truth, where understanding becomes lived experience.
The ego-self or lower soul. In Sufism, the nafs passes through progressive stages of purification, from the commanding nafs (ammara) to the contented nafs (mutma'inna).
The spiritual heart. In Sufi psychology, the seat of divine awareness and the organ of spiritual perception, distinct from the physical heart.
The spirit or divine breath within the human being. The deepest dimension of the self, connected directly to the divine source.
A spiritual state. A temporary condition of heightened spiritual awareness or divine quality that comes as grace, distinguished from a station (maqam) which is permanent.
A spiritual station. A stable, achieved level of spiritual development, in contrast to a hal (state) which is transient. The stations form the stages of the Sufi path.
Annihilation or passing away of the ego-self. The Sufi experience of dissolution of individual identity in the divine presence.
Subsistence or abiding. The stage following fana, where the mystic returns to ordinary life while maintaining continuous divine awareness.
Spiritual blessing or grace. The subtle spiritual energy transmitted through a lineage, a teacher, or sacred places and practices.
The chain of spiritual transmission linking a Sufi order back through its teachers to the Prophet Muhammad. The living lineage of blessing and authority.
The Sufi order founded by Hazrat Inayat Khan, continuing the transmission of universal Sufism. The parent organization of the Suluk Academy.
The mythical mountain at the edge of the world in Islamic cosmology. Mount Qaf symbolizes the ultimate goal of the spiritual journey. Used as the name for the Suluk class.
An honorific meaning 'Presence' or 'His/Her Holiness.' Used to address or refer to a revered spiritual figure, particularly Hazrat Inayat Khan.
Pir-o-Murshid Hazrat Inayat Khan (1882–1927). Indian musician and Sufi master who brought the Sufi message to the West in 1910. Founder of the Sufi Order.
The current Pir (head) of the Inayatiyya Order, grandson of Hazrat Inayat Khan. Principal teacher for the Suluk Academy Concentration curriculum.
Spiritual attentiveness or turning of the heart. The practice of directing one's inner attention toward a teacher, a quality, or the divine presence.
Divine qualities or attributes. The 99 Names of God in Islamic tradition, each representing an aspect of the divine nature available for contemplation and embodiment.
Witnessing or contemplative observation. An advanced Sufi practice of beholding divine manifestation in all things.
Spiritual listening or audition. The practice of deep listening to music, poetry, or the sounds of nature as a means of spiritual awakening.
A representative or successor. In Sufi orders, a khalif is authorized by the murshid to teach and guide students on the path.
A period of withdrawal from daily life for intensive spiritual practice. In the Sufi tradition, retreat (khalwa) involves seclusion, prayer, and meditation.
The formal entry into a Sufi order through a ceremony of commitment (bayat) between the student and the murshid, establishing the spiritual connection.
The oath of allegiance or initiation ceremony in Sufism. The formal commitment between a student and teacher that establishes the spiritual bond.
